records.ety: [F. revivere, L. revivere; pref. re- re- + vivere to live. See Vivid.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Revived (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Reviving.]

defs: To return to life; to recover life or strength; to live anew; to become reanimated or reinvigorated.
quotes: The Lord heard the voice of Elijah; and the soul of the child came into again, and he revived.

defs: Hence, to recover from a state of oblivion, obscurity, neglect, or depression; as, classical learning revived in the fifteenth century.
To recover its natural or metallic state, as a metal.

records.ety: [Cf. F. reviver. See Revive, v. i.]

defs: To restore, or bring again to life; to reanimate.
quotes: Those bodies, by reason of whose mortality we died, shall be revived.

defs: To raise from coma, languor, depression, or discouragement; to bring into action after a suspension.
quotes: Those gracious words revive my drooping thoughts.

defs: Hence, to recover from a state of neglect or disuse; as, to revive letters or learning.
To renew in the mind or memory; to bring to recollection; to recall attention to; to reawaken.
quotes: The mind has a power in many cases to revive perceptions which it has once had.

defs: To restore or reduce to its natural or metallic state; as, to revive a metal after calcination.
